text: Thoracolaparoscopic Oesophagectomy in prone position for Carcinoma Middle Third Oesophagus.
Comes from Gym Hospital, India.

The patient is positioned prone with supported chest and buttocks. A single lumen endotracheal tube is used for two-lung anesthesia. The scapula is pushed posteriorly, and a Veress needle is used to create a pneumothorax, resulting in a partial lung collapse.
The surgeon introduces a trocar at the angle of the seventh rib, followed by the placement of two additional trocars in the fifth and ninth intercostal spaces to achieve triangulation.
The lung is visibly collapsed due to pneumothorax, eliminating the need for traction during the thoracolaparoscopic esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon incises the mediastinal pleura both dorsally and ventrally over the tumor to facilitate en bloc removal.
The azygos vein is visualized, and the mediastinal pleura is incised at both ends, with the tissue being sculpted.
Following sculptinization, the surgeon applies silk sutures for double ligature on either side of the structure and proceeds to divide it.
The surgeon places the first ligature on the dorsal aspect, considering the direction of blood flow in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a second ligature on the ventral aspect of the esophagus, following the previous ligature on the dorsal side.
The surgeon uses a harmonic ACE device to apply a single ligature, which is visually adequate for the procedure.
The surgeon divides the vein, revealing the esophagus with no intervening structures visible between the surgical field and the esophagus.
The surgeon mobilizes the normal esophagus circumferentially, ensuring it is free from surrounding tissues at this level.
The surgeon uses a hook to coagulate the perforating vessels during the mobilization of the esophagus.
The surgeon places umbilical tape around the esophagus, securing it circumferentially.
The surgeon uses an instrument to apply traction on the esophagus, aiding in its mobilization during the procedure.
This can be performed with a surgeon or one additional trucker can be placed.
An assistant holds the retracted esophagus, maintaining its position as the surgeon continues the procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the site of the esophageal tumor and performs dissection around the area, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the tumor from surrounding tissues.
Umbilical tape is placed across the esophagus, providing traction on both the left side and the dorsal and ventral aspects.
The surgical field shows adequate exposure with the ventral bronchus clearly visualized. Lymph nodes are seen extending in continuity along the bronchus.
The surgeon clips and divides the tissue, likely around the site of the tumor, with assistance in retracting the esophagus.
The surgeon focuses on the left side of the esophagus, with visible retraction being assisted to maintain exposure.
The surgeon clips a chain of lymphatic vessels in continuity near the esophagus, with the tumor site previously visualized.
The surgeon mobilizes nodal tissues for removal, focusing on the left side of the esophagus and the dorsal and ventral aspects, with adequate exposure of the ventral bronchus.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the esophagus towards the neck thoracic inlet, dividing vessels using a hook or a 5mm curved harmonic scalpel, and performing an en bloc resection.
The surgeon performs a vagotomy, removing soft tissues and nodes along with the esophagus.
The surgeon carefully lifts the esophagus away from the trachea to protect the membranous portion of the trachea during the dissection.
The surgeon uses two-hand manipulation to depress the trachea downward while dividing the esophagus, taking care to avoid injury to the tracheal membrane.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the esophagus up to the inferior diaphragm, removing nodes along the right vagus nerve, and mobilizing the esophagus down to the crura of the diaphragm.
The assistant lifts the completely freed esophagus, exposing the mediastinal area in front of the surgeons for nodal clearance.
The surgeon begins dissection from the pericardium upwards towards the carina, removing the nodes and surrounding tissues in a single block.
The surgeon carefully removes tissue clips from the site near the left bronchus, ensuring complete clearance of the area.
The surgeon completes the infracarinal nodal dissection, ensuring all targeted lymph nodes are removed from the area beneath the carina.
The tracheal bifurcation and both bronchi are clearly visualized, with surrounding anatomical structures exposed following the nodal dissection.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of supracarinal nodes at the level of the aorta, carefully separating the nodal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon picks up the dissected nodes, which have been removed in one block from the pericardium to the carina.
The right lacrymogen is visualized adjacent to the tracheal bifurcation and bronchus during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection at the thoracic inlet, focusing on the surrounding anatomical structures.
After completing the nodal clearance, the surgeon ensures the esophagus is free and checks for hemostasis. The patient is then repositioned to a prone position.
The patient is positioned supine, and the surgeon performs gastric mobilization using a five-port technique.
The surgeon incises the lesser omentum, revealing underlying structures and facilitating further access to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left gastric vein and excises lymph nodes from the celiac region and the right common hepatic artery area.
The surgeon divides the left gastric vein using a harmonic scalpel, with a clip placed before division when using alternative energy sources.
The surgeon picks up the lymph nodes, likely for further dissection or removal, following the completion of nodal clearance and esophagus mobilization.
The surgeon divides the right left gastric artery and continues the dissection, further mobilizing the gastric tissue.

The surgeon clips the left gastric vein for safety and continues the dissection from the upper border of the pancreas towards the hilum of the spleen. The dissection includes removal of nodes along the splenic artery and soft tissues up to the diaphragm.

The surgeon mobilizes the lower end of the esophagus by dividing the renal esophageal membrane. The fundus is released from the diaphragm, facilitating easier dissection due to prior work completed in the chest.
The surgeon rotates the pancreas downward to expose and clear the lymph nodes along the celiac axis.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect the gastrocolic omentum, dividing the veins between the colon and the greater curvature while carefully preserving the gastroepiploic pedicle.
The surgeon continues the division of the short gastric vessels, completing the dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon demonstrates the completely freed fundus, having been released from the diaphragm and surrounding structures.
The surgeon visualizes the hiatus and divides the gastrocolic omentum up to the second part, near the head of the pancreas.
The surgeon completes the tension-free mobilization of the stomach, ensuring it is adequately freed from surrounding structures.
The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the neck, identifies and divides the esophagus, and secures a rail tube to the lower end of the esophagus.
The surgeon attaches a rail tube or smooth plastic tube to the lower end of the esophagus and pushes it into the mediastinum, facilitating the esophageal mobilization.
The surgeon pulls the entire esophagus, along with the attached rail tube, into the peritoneal cavity, completing the mobilization of the esophagus.
The surgeon visualizes the tumor within the surgical field, likely located near the esophagus or stomach, as part of the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon displays a Riles tube, which is used in the procedure.
This riles tube is going to be used for the gastric pull-up at the later stage.
The surgeon makes a midline incision extending over the antrum, allowing the exteriorization of the esophagus, riles tube, and stomach.
The surgeon resects the stomach along the lesser curvature up to the fundus, creating a gastric tube with a diameter of five to six centimeters, which is slightly wider than usual.
The surgical team displays the resected specimen, which includes the portion of the stomach and esophagus removed during the procedure.
The gastric tube is visualized reaching the neck without tension, extending through the posterior mediastinum.
The surgeon performs a running stitch along the staple line of the gastric tube. A pyloromyotomy is also conducted through the same midline incision.
The surgeon reattaches the Ryles tube to the apex of the gastric tube and uses it to pull the gastric tube up into the posterior mediastinum.

The surgeon checks for any gastric twist and narrows the hiatus, securing the stomach to the hiatus crura with two or three stitches to prevent herniation of the omentum or bowel into the right pleural cavity. A tube is placed as part of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the duodenal jejunal flexure and creates a jejunostomy, visually establishing a connection between the jejunum and another part of the digestive tract.
The surgeon removes the esophagus and performs nodal clearance in the middle third of its vessel.
